section_title: INTRODUCTION TO THE ICT JUDAS SWING
content:
This teaching is focused on understanding the ICT Judas Swing.
Key concepts to be introduced and defined:
- The ICT Judas Swing itself.
- Its utilization in bullish conditions.
- Its utilization in bearish conditions.

section_title: DEFINITION AND HIGHER TIME FRAME CONTEXT
content:
The price swing that sets the London session high or low is many times an energetic price swing. This can
often be a run on stops or a return to a key price level. Either can be the basis for this quick spike in price.
It is crucial to have a higher time frame understanding and implement higher time frame analysis to
determine what direction the ICT Judas Swing will form. This will help solidify the understanding that
creates the daily high and low, the open, high, low, and close on a daily candle.
Starting with the lowest timeframe possible, such as a small segment of price action from an intraday
perspective, allows for understanding that these ideas are fractal and form on all time frames.

- TIMING AND CORE CONCEPT
First and foremost, you start hunting the Judas Swing at midnight New York time. This is the New York
opening price and it ends at 5:00 a.m. New York time. This entire five-hour window is utilized for anticipating
this price move.
Between midnight New York time and 5:00 a.m. New York time, there's typically going to be a high or low
formed for the daily range, usually on the basis of a London high or London low forming.
The Judas Swing is a false run that trips traders who lack the understanding of the true direction of the day.
This understanding comes by way of higher time frame analysis and implementing that into the intraday
price action.

- IMPLEMENTATION OVERVIEW
Here is an example of a Judas Swing implemented in bullish conditions, using the Euro Dollar chart.
On the chart, the Asian range is applied, along with the midnight delineation in New York. The opening
price at midnight New York time (referred to as New York open price) is highlighted and typically drawn out
to around 11 o'clock in the morning. The Asian range high and low are also visible.
The premise for this setup requires an existing analysis that strongly suggests bullish prices for Euro Dollar
for the particular day. If this assumption is maintained, and you only take trades based on the suggested
criteria in a demo account, you will observe a repeating phenomenon and consistency.
This setup will not provide a trade every single day, but it will appear a few times a week across major pairs
coupled with the dollar, such as Euro Dollar, Cable, Aussie Dollar, and Dollar Yen. Watching these four
markets offers a wide array of potential scenarios where this pattern forms.

- MECHANICS AND ENTRY
In a bullish scenario, the ICT Judas Swing is identified through specific price action:
- Look for an Asian range that is in a tight consolidation.
- Look for an initial run-up to trip traders, hitting buy stops, making traders go long on a breakout.
- Wait for the market to then trade down below the Asian range low to take stops.
- Anticipate a sweep of 10-20 pips or so below the Asian range low. This move below the opening price is
actually the ICT Judas Swing.
This is a bullish Judas Swing, and it typically creates the low of the day inside the London session, which
ends up becoming the low of the actual day.
What to look for:
- A higher time frame premise anticipating bullishness or a higher close on the day.
- Wait for a scenario where the Asian range is initially tripped by running stops above the Asian range high.
This is the first level of manipulation.
- Anticipate a move below the opening price, preferably trading below the Asian range low. Trading just
below that Asian range low can serve as an entry point, using a respectable amount of pips for a stop. This
can lead to a good trade, potentially towards support levels or old highs, classic support/resistance ideas
can apply here.
The actual drop below the opening price, when the market is bullish, is the ICT Judas Swing. Any small
movement below the opening price without the preceding manipulation is not a Judas Swing.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING THE TRUE BULLISH JUDAS SWING AND THE JUDAS GOAT
content:
ANALOGY
The Judas Swing requires specific elements to be present; otherwise, it's not a Judas Swing. The Judas
Swing is first designed to lead traders in on the wrong side, then it tears lower, going below the Asian
range low and below the opening price. This whole move below the opening price is exactly the ICT Judas
Swing.
The concept is named after the 'Judas Goat' analogy: sheep herders would use a Judas goat to lead sheep
into the slaughterhouse, but the Judas goat would turn away just before entering, while the sheep
continued to their fate.
In price action, this premise is seen where:
- They introduce a false breakout (the initial pop above the Asian range high).
- Then, the 'Judas goat' leads price action lower (the drop below the opening price and Asian range low).
- Traders seeing this want to go short, selling, selling, selling, which is actually the wrong thing to do
because the actual price is going the other way after it goes below the opening price.

- ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS
The key elements of a bullish Judas Swing are:
- A pop above the Asian range high.
- Followed by a drop below the opening price at midnight.
- And then a drop below the Asian range low.
When this sequence occurs, that is the actual Judas Swing that indicates bullishness. Visualizing these
elements clearly on the price action provides significant clarity as to why price is moving the way it is.

- IMPLEMENTATION OVERVIEW
Now, let's define the ICT Judas Swing for a bearish scenario, using the Dollar CAD as an example. The
idea going into this day would be higher timeframe bearishness, anticipating a lower close based on daily
or weekly analysis.
Similar to the bullish setup:
- The Asian range is a small, tight consolidation.
- We want to see it initially drop down, tripping traders in right away (leading them to sell).
- Then, it runs on the other side, above the Asian range high.
That movement above the opening price and above the Asian range high is the Judas Swing. This is the
false or trap move often seen in London setups. While this is not the only setup, it is a key characteristic of
the setups used.
By looking through price charts and creating a study log of what these individual days look like in hindsight,
even if not trading live, you can build a historical reference in your memory. This will help you start to see
when it should be happening again when watching live price action.

- MECHANICS AND TRAPS
The number one premise when anticipating a bearish Judas Swing is to have a higher time frame analysis
that suggests lower prices or bearish momentum.
After midnight New York time, we want to see price initially drop down below the Asian range low. When
that happens, we wait and anticipate the run back above the Asian range high. When price does that and
goes above the opening price, at that moment, this becomes a Judas Swing.
It is leading people in on the wrong side because our higher time frame analysis already suggests a
bearish tone and lower prices. Any rally intraday at this time of day should be viewed as a suspect rally or a
fake move.
This move will run up, get everybody excited (e.g., breakout traders going long), and then the actual prices
will go the other way. This run above the opening price is where short holders who were trading a breakout
below the Asian range low would have their stops hit (typically 10-20 pips above).

section_title: IDENTIFYING A VALID BEARISH JUDAS SWING
content:
The Judas Swing will typically reach for traits (likely 'targets' or 'levels'), form the high of the day, and then
quickly move away. This quick move away is a telltale hallmark sign that traders have now been trapped.
Any subsequent return back to the Asian range high does not provide an opportunity to see higher prices,
and then price quickly accelerates going lower in the New York session.
To identify a nice ICT Judas Swing, the elements and characteristics are very simple:
- You want to see the manipulation first (e.g., when bearish, below the Asian range low).
- Then, a climb above the opening price at midnight.
- And finally, a move through the Asian range high.

- SUMMARY AND BACKTESTING
Once price executes the sequence of manipulation below the Asian range low, climbs above the midnight
opening price, and goes through the Asian range high, that's a Judas Swing. You can demo trade that or
perform backtesting to see how this works out.
Many times, when you have a bearish tone from a higher time frame perspective, this is the type of move
you will see. Another classic sign that your assessment is correct is if price starts to move aggressively
away from that high, because it has already 'done its work' and trapped traders (the 'sheep are getting
slaughtered').
Looking at a large sampling of data (e.g., three or four months across a couple of pairs) will demonstrate
that this phenomenon actually happens in the marketplace. It's a repeating, classic phenomenon in London
setups, exhibiting consistency.
However, it's important not to believe that it will form every single day. There will be times when a setup is
strongly in your favor to see either trades lower (with a lower close) or a higher close (when bullish).

section_title: HIGH PROBABILITY TRADING WITH THE JUDAS SWING
content:
The key takeaway from this teaching is that if you do not see this specific phenomenon (the Judas Swing)
take place, it is probably better for you to stay out of the marketplace.
This is because you want to be trading in the highest probable conditions. In my opinion, if you are looking
for London setups, the ICT Judas Swing is one of the best things to look for and a hallmark of outstanding
London sessions.
